@fhieber I reverted the ``--input-constraints`` option and covered the other small issues listed above.

Another solution is to have both source and target max length options.  That would let us cover very unbalanced cases without all the extra buckets.

One issue with relying too much on the length ratio is that it's a hard cut-off for anything over the average.  For instance, if the average ratio is 0.7, we max out at (100, 70).  Examples with lower ratios such as (100, 60) are allowed while those with higher ratios such as (100, 75) aren't.  This biases our training data in a way that we probably don't want, especially since neural models already have problems producing too-short translations.

Getting around this would require pre-calculating the average ratio plus some allowance (how much?) for deviation, and scaling max_seq_len appropriately.  Even if we did this, fitting an example (100, 90) from above would require buckets up to (130, 91), wasting space on the source side.  The idea behind filling in buckets would be to add (100, 77) ... (100, 91) to better fit examples.

I think ratio plus fill-in is good for most tasks since translation tends to have ratios between 0.5 and 2.  For more unbalanced cases, we can add separate source/target max lengths.

@aehlke serverless functions can be replaced with 3 resources: `AWS::Lambda::Function`, `AWS::IAM::Role` and `AWS::Lambda::Permission`, then you just customize them as needed:
```yaml
  Function:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Environment:
        Variables:
          VAR_NAME: !Ref ...
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
      Code:
        S3Bucket: !Ref LambdaBucket
        S3Key: !Ref LambdaPackageKey
      Role: !GetAtt FunctionRole.Arn

  FunctionRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: 2012-10-17
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service: lambda.amazonaws.com
            Action: sts:AssumeRole
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: FunctionRole
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: 2012-10-17
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - service:Action
                Resource:
                  - !GetAtt Resource.Arn

  FunctionRolePermission:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Permission
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Ref Function
      Action: lambda:InvokeFunction
      Principal: apigateway.amazonaws.com
      SourceArn:
        !Sub arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:${ApiId}/${ApiStage}/GET/...
```
Before execution of the stack, you also need to upload lambda package as a zip file to `s3://${LambdaBucket}/${LambdaPackageKey}`.

So my first stab at what I think we should do (and, not working through this problem myself, I might be wrong), is to remove the hard-coded names in `sql_result.py`, and instead refer to constants on the backend's class for those names. The defaults would be `'MODIFY COLUMN'` and `'ADD COLUMN'`, but your backend could change those to `'ALTER'` and `'ADD'`. The list could then be updated to refer to those.

That way, the `op` remains what it is today, the actual `ALTER TABLE` operation being performed. The backend is in complete control of the name.

If even more specialized logic/formatting is needed, I'd then suggest to break out that bit of logic further into a function that the backend can override in order to better prepare its `ALTER TABLE` statement.

Just a few points:
* My opinion is that running all the tests for every PR is not bloat, but is rather using a test server precisely as intended. If anything, we should run a limited set of tests *locally* before pushing (if the test suit gets large enough to warrant it) and let the test server do the heavy work.
* IMO link checking is of marginal utility, which is why I'm happy to only run it occasionally. But I'm opposed to only running *tests* occasionally. It creates the possibility of catching bugs too late.
* Wanting to skip tests is a *syptom* of a poor test suite -- but Kolibri doesn't have a poor test suite. In fact, it's only got a handful of tests at all, and they run really fast and aren't brittle. When we introduce tests that have a large # of possible failure points (like browser tests with Selenium) let's be conscientious enough to design *easily testable* systems. (One thing that makes KA Lite's browser tests brittle is that the design is not loosely coupled, making it testable *only with difficulty*.)

Remember [This is not KA Lite](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1s8kqh1NSbHlzPCtaI1AbIsLsgGH3bopYbZdM1RzgxN8/edit#heading=h.c2zyvhwke9ry).

```
>>> policy = {
...   "Id": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:12345:queue-name/SQSDefaultPolicy", 
...   "Statement": [
...     {
...       "Action": [
...         "SQS:SendMessage"
...       ],
...       "Condition": {
...         "ArnEquals": {
...           "aws:SourceArn": "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789123:sns-topic"
...         }
...       },
...       "Effect": "Allow",
...       "Principal": {
...         "AWS": "*"
...       },
...       "Resource": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:12345:queue-name",
...       "Sid": "Sid12345"
...     }
...   ] , 
...   "Version":  "2012-10-17"
... }
>>> policy = Policy(policy)
>>> policy.whos_allowed()
set([Principal(category='principal', value='*'), Condition(category='arn', value='arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789123:sns-topic')])

False
```

Hi zeeshanafzal,

django-pyodbc is built on top of pyodbc, which uses unixODBC.  Per our readme:


>  You may need to configure your machine and drivers to do an ODBC connection to your database server, if you haven't already. For Linux this involves installing and [configuring Unix ODBC and FreeTDS .](http://www.unixodbc.org/doc/FreeTDS.html) Iterate on the command line to test your pyodbc connection like:
> 
>     python -c 'import pyodbc; print(pyodbc.connect("DSN=foobar_mssql_data_source_name;UID=foo;PWD=bar").cursor().execute("select 1"))'
> 

So, per the readme, please go to http://www.unixodbc.org/doc/FreeTDS.html to figure out your odbc connection and then modify the above `python -c ...` command to test it on the command line.

This is not a django-pyodbc issue, so I am closing.  Feel free to comment more in order to receive help, if we can, but this is a unixODBC issue, not a django-pyodbc issue.  

Good luck!

> Any further thoughts on this naming

I don't have a good name right now.

> Could the field value ever be a `uri` but not a `urn`?

Yes! The reason why we used this style in the first place rather than keeping headers and payload hashes under separate attributes, so that we can generalize it. If a record is stored on an HTTP URL we can use that directly or if a content is to be fetched from WARC file we can have something like `urin:warcs/{offset}/{length}/{warc_file_name_or_path}/`. So, it was a generalization effort.

> Once we change this name, should we have some adaptation considerations for older versions of ipwb that used `locator`?

Changing this name is about standardizing terminologies used in CDXJ files for archival indexing purposes, irrespective of the tool they are used in. Once such a change is made, we will have a few choices: 1) have an fallback keyword in the replay to look for the old name for a while, 2) provide a migration script/command that changes old CDXJ files in the new style, or 3) if the user base of the tool is small, we can just introduce this breaking change and inform in the release not and the README file.

## Another alternative approach would be to:

* Try for https://ibnesayeed.github.io/acrts/ instead and explain that all-green archived version is captured in [a WARC file](https://github.com/ibnesayeed/acrts/blob/master/assets/acrts.warc.gz). (The replay might need some work to fix the routing issues.)
* Run the indexer that would push the content in local IPFS instance and generate the CDXJ (index) file.
* Share the newly generated index file with participants, but not the content, and ask them to run IPWB locally using that index.
* Someone else (potentially me) can then run IPWB and demonstrate the all-green archived page. Handing it over to a different person on a separate machine under a separate account is for the greater impact for the observer which would not require an explanation of what we did to leverage the distributed discovery of the underlying IPFS.

[CLAPS]

We can then talk about the proposed new model that would require exchanging the index file, in fact it won't require an index file at all. This would allow us to encourage people to prioritize the work on making historical records of IPNS changes, potentially, by using Ethereum blockchain.

## Things to do before demo:

* Fix any issues with ServiceWorker implementation to improve replay.
* Write a Dockerfile and Docker Stack file to easily run an instance anywhere by anyone.

> The daemon ports can be customized by a user so ought to be parameterized.

You can put them in ENV vars and use those variables instead. However, docker-compose files are meant to be customized as necessary and then used. Compose files are nothing but a handy way of storing all the parameters in a well structured format that you would otherwise write in one or more long flag decorated docker run (and some other) commands.

I'll take over this one.

Based on the lesson I learned from [this thread](https://github.com/fonttools/fonttools/pull/891), I'm writing the following design plan, discuss it with fellows, before actually starting to code. 

Please correct me if I'm wrong. Or please even stop me if it involves to much work and I need to focus on other higher priority stuff.

**Goal**

Respect 'Filter' parameter in fontmake pipeline.

**Background**
First, According to [Glyphs HandBook](https://glyphsapp.com/content/1-get-started/2-manuals/1-handbook-glyphs-2-0/Glyphs-Handbook-2.3.pdf#page=184) page 184, Filter is a glyphapp specific custom parameter.

Second, In glyphsLib [builder.py](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/master/Lib/glyphsLib/builder.py#L369) line 369, we dump all non-OpenType table entries (including glyphapp specific custom parameter) into ufo.lib, with a [GLYPHS_PREFIX](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/master/Lib/glyphsLib/builder.py#L38) inserted at the start of the name.
`ufo.lib[GLYPHS_PREFIX + name] = value`

Finally, neither [GlyphsLib](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib) nor [Fontmake](https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake) respects the Filter value later, so as many other other parameters. 

For all glyphsApp specific custom parameters (those whose name are capitalized, for example: Master Name, Color Palettes are glyphsApp specific, but hheaDescender, underlineThickness are UFO spcific), we only respect Don't use Production Names, Glyphs.Export and Keep Glyphs so far. So all other glyphsApp specific custom parameters takes no effect in our pipeline (This list might not be accurate). I would recommend the designer not use these custom parameters for now.

**Overview**
As the Glyphs Handbook page 184 says,

> Filter: Triggers Glyphs filters or app functions in an instance, **after decomposition of compound glyphs.** 

The decomposition of compound glyphs happens in [fontmake.font_project.decompose_glyphs ](https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/blob/master/Lib/fontmake/font_project.py#L96) and is out of GlyphsLibs's control.

My plan is to **add a function in GlyphsLib called 'apply_custom_parameter_filter(ufo)'**, which takes an ufo as parameter, read ufo.lib[GLYPHS_PREFIX + 'Filter'], analyze it and apply it to glyphs in ufo. 

And fontmake (probably other applications) calls this function at a proper time.

**Detailed Design**

Modify UFO glyphs according to the filter so that we can safely remove filter parameter afterwards. I think I need to read [Defcon](https://github.com/typesupply/defcon/) as well as [UFO 3 Spec](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/index.html) to figure out which value should I modify. Any advises are appreciate if someone can point out which specific value do I need to modify or which specific section do I need to read regarding to applying the filter.

**Extensible**
We can create more functions to handle other custom parameters and finally bundle them together into a single apply_custom_parameter.py file. 

PS: the filter contains following elements
 • AddExtremes
 • HatchOutlineFilter; OriginX:x; OriginY:y;
StepWidth:distance; Angle:angle; Offset:thickness
 • OffsetCurve; x; y; make stroke; position/auto
 • RemoveOverlap
 • Roughenizer; segment length; x; y; angle
 • RoundCorner; radius; visual correction
 • RoundedFont; stem
 • Transformations; LSB:±*/shift; RSB:±*/shift;
Width:±shift; ScaleX:percent; ScaleY:percent;
Slant:amount; SlantCorrection:bool; OffsetX:amount;
OffsetY:amount; Origin:select

There are already explicit calls to ``.use_asyncio()`` in the ``autobahn.asyncio.wamp`` package -- but if anything else imports something out of ``autobahn.twisted.wamp`` then that will call ``.use_twisted()`` and "win". That is: you can't mix twisted + asyncio in one process.

So, I think the best thing to do is make either of the ``.use_*()`` calls "one-time" in txaio and have it throw an exception if you try to use something different (e.g. calling ``.use_asyncio()`` twice is okay, but calling ``.use_twisted()`` after gives you an exception).

Hey @cbeer, I think this is awesome. Ideally, in my view, we should merge these two features (basic auth, and bulk query) into the original collector.

I'm interested to know how many beans is "many", in your case. And, is the primary cost savings because of less round trips, or because you are querying far fewer beans now (via whitelist). What would happen if this implementation asked for "all" beans in the bulk query by default? 

The original jolokia collector does a listing of available beans, then it goes domain by domain to fetch the full set of data for each. It doesn't actually do a query per bean, although I can see how this could still be a lot of requests. I don't know how large the result set would be, but we could easily update the original jolokia collector to fetch all beans in 2 requests. One for the listing (to get the list of domains), and a bulk request to fetch all beans for all domains.

The original jolokia collector does all filtering on the collector side. I like how your implementation let's jolokia do the work. I think this makes a lot more sense, especially for a specific whitelist of mbeans. I would also prefer "querying" jolokia via wildcard syntax versus doing regex matches after the fact. I'm not a huge fan of the regex matching as implemented today, although I could see the argument for it if jolokia's wildcards aren't expressive enough. 

Thoughts?

> All cast functions return strings for errors. It means it is "impossible" to use the cast functions alone, as exception handling now requires a strict dependency on the Field class

Cast functions is not a part of public API.

> There is now a "legacy" package which is still in use? How is it still required, considering the the above refactor

Yes. It's used by `infer` function. Haven't touched it for now per https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tableschema-py/issues/105#issuecomment-288161154

> We now use six (another refactoring?), so do we still need the simpler compat module?

`six` is a standard accros all our python code so it has been used by default for all newly written `isinstance(value, six.string_types)` (see below in type related changes section). `compat` is still in use in some place - will be handled in #83. That's just not a focus of this PR.   

> I can't see where you cast constraint values, so for example if someone had constraints like '3' or '2017-01-01' these will fail, as your new check functions work on the assumption of values already cast. Or, have I missed something in here?

It's covered here - https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tableschema-py/blob/null-types-constraints-v1/tableschema/field.py#L153-L167 (and cast function preparation above). `Table/Schema/Field` passes all pre-PR tests not related to spec-v1 changes.

That's change is really important because existent code was making `goodtables.py` and `GTIO` to work really ineffective. For example for table with 20 columns and 10 000 rows this code `Field.__prepare_cast_function` and `Field.__prepare_checks_functions` (including constraint casting) was called for every `Field.cast_value` call means 200 000 calls.

> I don't see any changes made to foreign key support. That may be ok, but please confirm.

That's correct. Per https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tableschema-py/issues/33#issuecomment-288160861 it will be covered later - #33. On `Schema` level it's already compliant to spec-v1 after #157.

> Previously, if a format was invalid or simply not handled in the current implementation, then the default format would be used. Now, there is no default casting handler (as casting is not based on inheritance), and default is not consistently a fallback in each casting function (for example here an unknown format keyword will eventually raise an error, yet here default, or any other unknown keyword will pass through, consistent with the previous implementation). Am I missing something in the control flow?

Now it's an invariant provided by spec - https://specs.frictionlessdata.io/schemas/table-schema.json - valid descriptor can't have not-supported `format`.

---

Changes related to field in this PR:
- `missingValues` moved from field level to schema level and set to `['']`
- it's hard to say what's exactly changed to null/types/constraints implementation because even with my knowledge of codebase I was hardly understanding what's really happening with value (esp. null values management) - now it's just compliant to the spec (including our latest discussion about constraints casting)
- removed duplication of constraints implementation (in `constraints` folder and `Field.__check_*`) which has been introduced because initial types system doesn't work for `goodtables-py` on granular constraint checks

---

Changes related to types in PR:
- general
  - all types when applicable wasn't compliant to spec with handling cast values of other types e.g. `IntegerField.cast_value(3.14) == 3` instead of `IntegerField.cast_value(3.14) == ERROR`. Now all types accept only string (lexical representation) or cast value of proper type.
  - types `duration`, `year` and `yearmonth` has been added by side contributor earlier (#152) so only changed after this PR is covered by this diff
- date
  - changed format=`fmt:<PATTERN>` to `format=<PATTERN>`
- datetime
  - changed format=`fmt:<PATTERN>` to `format=<PATTERN>`
- geojson
  - added support for `format=topojson` without validation (#158)
- number
  - now currency symbols will be removed only if there is `currency=True`
  - not changed in spec but it was not compliant:
    - fixed default `groupChar` to `''` from `','` (may be we're better change spec instead if it's really breaking to OS cc @akariv )
    - fixed handling values with percent symbols - now it will be divided by 100
- time
  - changed format=`fmt:<PATTERN>` to `format=<PATTERN>`
- year
  - now checked to have length == 4 as a string 
  - now checked to be inside [0, 9999] as an integer
  
---

> refactoring that is purely aesthetic (even taste driven - OO to functional).

Just can't agree on it - reducing complexity, improving testability and effectiveness is not an aesthetic changes. Esp. before porting this library to other 8 languages. Even I with all my knowledge of the codebase wasn't sure what's needed to be done to be sure that `Field` is null/types/constraints compliant to spec-v1 without simplifying things.

> How to apply this patch to an existing cluster?

Honestly, I'm not sure how to back out of the changes we've made on a running cluster. I'd try the following:

* `sudo systemctl stop timedate-ntp-synchronization.service`
* `sudo systemctl stop ntpdate.service`
* `sudo systemctl mask timedate-ntp-synchronization.service`
* Remove `/etc/systemd/system/ntpd.service.d/debug.conf`
* `sudo systemctl stop systemd-timesyncd.service`
* `sudo systemctl restart ntpd.service`

If that gets you in a state where `ntpd` stays running happily, I think that's a success.

Connection pool is a reuse of TCP connection to host, so it should improve situation a bit making less TCP handshakes. But it won't help you with headers if this was your task.

Btw there is a better way to add connection pooling support as far as I understand - just using Session from `requests` and [HTTPAdapter](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/api/#requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter):

```
import requests

sess = requests.Session()

adapter = requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter(pool_connections=5, pool_maxsize=5)
sess.mount('http://', adapter)
sess.mount('https://', adapter)

resp = sess.post("http://localhost:8000/api/")
```

This way we don't introduce new dependency as `urllib3` is already part of `requests`. But the question is do we really need it in `cent`?

> fetch_candidate_head will apply getNextLog;

Technically, it's `guess_head` that you want to do here, not `fetch_candidate_head`. `fetch_candidate_head` is a method that just outputs a possible head; `guess_head` _uses_ `fetch_candidate_head` but then tries to check that block's ancestors

> [JSON-RPC] Before executing txs, the shard client needs some information from main chain:
256-previous-main-chain-blockhashes;

Note that this is required not just to make your own collation, but also to verify previous collations as part of `guess_head`.

"Environments" are one of the main features of Conda. I recommend to read up on that in the user guide: https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/concepts.html#conda-environments

If you take a look at https://anaconda.org/ccordoba12/repo, you can see that there is not only `pcl` but also `python-pcl` -- I guess you want
```bat
conda install -c ccordoba12 --name pcl python-pcl
```
(note that Conda is not a pure Python package manager, but a more generic one; hence sometimes (to avoid collisions) Python packages may have slightly different names as those you know from PyPI.)

This is one of those times where my limited experience with standard Python development practices comes in handy. I probably don't know what I'm missing.

I tend to develop right in the source directory. I have a Jupyter notebook that I use test out individual routines, with key modules loaded in cell 1 and either a call to `api.get_index()` or code to load `tests/index.json`. When I need to run the commands from the shell, I just substitute `python conda/cli/main.py` for `conda`.

Every once in awhile, I find myself confused by Python's path resolution behavior. Usually, just the fact that I'm sitting in the conda source directory is enough to ensure I'm getting the right version of the files. Other times, I have to set PYTHONPATH. But occasionally, even that isn't enough, and what I do is manually rename the installed `conda` package directory to, say, `conda2`, so I can be sure Python doesn't find it. I usually don't forget to move it back.

Hi,
- I am using admin-bootstrapped 2.5.7 with django 1.8.8.
- I don't think this is a regression.
django-bootstrapped is a very convenient, more modern look for the django project. I personally feel it should be the default look for django-admin. It is a petty it requires some fiddling before having a pixel perfect replacement for the default django-admin look. 

Django-admin considers the first column to be the label for the second column, so the first column-header applies to the second column. So this makes the default bootstrapped admin look funny for the tabular layout.

An alternative would be to provide a way to set the column header for the first column. So that each column get its own header.

@kdubezerra 

So you want to remove not older, but newer indices?

Your config might look like this:

```
---
actions:
  1:
    action: delete_indices
    description: Delete the newest 10 indices by creation_date
    options:
      ignore_empty_list: True
      continue_if_exception: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: count
      count: 10
      reverse: False
      use_age: True
      source: creation_date
```

Please keep in mind that this is a _very_ simplistic configuration.  There are no other filters, so this will delete _any_ index recently created, including `.kibana` and the `.marvel-*` indices.  You should probably prepend the count filter with a pattern filter to select only indices matching a pattern.

Out of idle curiosity, why would you want to delete the _newest_ indices instead of older ones?  What's the use case?

The timestamp's timezone produced by Curator (Great job on 4.0 btw) is in the CET timezone (Same as the server). When collecting logs using Filebeat (Which I assumed most users do) it expects the timestamp to be in UTC. So in Eleasticsearch the events are showing up 6 hours behind when they acutally happened. See Filebeat docs about @timestamp field [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/beats/filebeat/current/exported-fields-env.html#_timestamp).

You may find the following sketch helpful for serializing. It's not production-ready or stream-friendly, and it waits until the result is finished to convert to a pd DataFrame. This version is notebook-friendly in that interrupting a cell in a notebook does interrupt the cell. 

 ```
import pandas as pd
import prestodb

class PrestoConnector():

    def __init__(self, hostname="ahost", port=8000, user="pyPresto", catalog="hive", schema="mySchema"):
        """
        An interface for running queries from a PrestoDB cluster running on a certain domain and port.
        """
        self.hostname=hostname
        self.port=port
        self.user=user
        self.catalog=catalog
        self.schema=schema

    def query(self, input_query):
        """Executes the query and returns a Pandas DataFrame of the results."""
        try:
            conn=prestodb.dbapi.connect( host=self.hostname,
                                              port=self.port,
                                              user=self.user,
                                              catalog=self.catalog,
                                              schema=self.schema,
                                              isolation_level=1  #Allows us to cancel queries
                               )
            cur = conn.cursor()
            cur.execute(input_query)
            query_results = cur.fetchall()
            colnames = [part[0] for part in cur.description]
            cur.cancel()
            conn.close()
            df = pd.DataFrame(query_results, columns=colnames)
            print ("The dataframe has {} rows of {} columns.".format(df.shape[0],df.shape[1]))
            return df
        except:
            cur.cancel()
            conn.close()
            raise
```

The reason I chose to change btVector3 to btVector, is because the naming seams to be wrong in Bullet itself.
Bullet use btVector3 both for 3D and 4D vector. btVector4 derive btVector3, and it's bad, IMHO, for 2 reasons:
1. btVector4 is not btVector3 (against the IsA rule of inheritance)
2. Inheritance of such elementary type is one of the obstacles for C API

Therefore, the first thing I should have done is to change btVector3 to btVector, and then tell other classes(btVector3&btVector4) to derive from it.

I suspect that Amazon is passing you a cross-signed certificate. Per [this blog post](https://lukasa.co.uk/2015/04/Certifi_State_Of_Union/) I wrote in April, cross-signed roots represent a problem for older versions of OpenSSL because versions of OpenSSL prior to 1.0.2 are not able to trust non-self-signed root certificates.

Also per that blog post, the most recent version of certifi primarily contains a bundle that does not include the cross-signed roots. If you try replacing your call to `certifi.where()` with `certifi.old_where()`, you should find that everything keeps working as it was before.

However, please note that at some time in the next few months `certifi.old_where()` will be removed entirely. Prior to this time you should either upgrade to OpenSSL 1.0.2 (which on my machine encounters no problems using the current certifi bundle with https://amazon.com/) or pressure Amazon to stop supplying a cross-signed root certificate.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

You calculate the reference label like this:
`label = numpy.argmax(fmodel.predictions(image))`,
i.e. with RGB color channel ordering, but the network expects BGR and you also use BGR when running the attack: `adversarial = attack(image[:,:,::-1], label, unpack=False)`
Therefore, the reference label and the prediction for the input image made by the attack differ and the original image is _misclassified_.

In other words: just use `label = numpy.argmax(fmodel.predictions(image[:, :, ::-1]))` to get the reference label.

However, I think Foolbox should abstract away the color channel ordering (RGB vs. BGR) as part of the preprocessing so that the user does not run into these issues. I will probably add this to the next Foolbox release. Thanks @neale!


P.S.: when I run the code in your gist, I do get a warning that helped me track down this problem:
> .../foolbox/attacks/base.py:93: UserWarning: Not running the attack because the original image is already misclassified and the adversarial thus has a distance of 0.

Do you get this warning as well, @neale, or is it hidden because of your python configuration? In that case, it would be good to know so that we can make sure that users see the warnings.

Something I'd like to resolve is whether and how applications should be able to specify the ordering of several marshallers. Of course there are many ways to accomplish this, or we could simply say developers shouldn't write marshallers so that they depend on one anothers' outputs. Anyway, this got me thinking: since the names of the methods have no real significance, is the automatic identification of all "marshal_" methods even that useful — as opposed to just a single master marshalling method? Having only one method to execute (which may or may not be split into several subroutines, of course) would take the ordering question out of the equation.

@UberFarmer — I don't think there's anything to stop you from using regular Python properties and the *serialized_name* parameter on the field to accomplish what the SA code is doing. Except, of course, that the accessor methods are not applied during import or export, which may be what you're asking for here. And I agree this is something to be addressed, since the (un)marshallers are supposed to affect import/export *only*. If there's a transformation that should be *always* applied (on import/export as well as when application code accesses the attribute), then I think the code should be placed in some Schematics-specific accessor just like SA's *hybrid_property*. This way all three cases would be covered:
*    to transform on attribute access only, use regular property
*    to transform on import/export only, use marshaller
*    to transform always, use magic property.

Am I making sense here?

@omgq The best way depends on the details of the situation, which are kind of lacking in your example.

One obvious method is to create distinct models via composition. If there are other accompanying fields that are specific to the `'a'` or `'b'` variant, this approach probably makes the most sense.

```python
class Container(Model):
    type = StringType()
    # Other common fields go here.

    def __new__(cls, raw_data={}, *args, **kwargs):
        submodel = raw_data.get('type')
        class_ = {'a': AContainer, 'b': BContainer}.get(submodel, cls)
        return super(Container, cls).__new__(class_, raw_data, *args, **kwargs)


class AMixin(Model):
    a = ModelType(A)
    # Other 'a'-specific fields go here.


class AContainer(Container, AMixin):
    pass

...
```

```python
>>> Container({'type': 'a'})
<AContainer instance>
>>> Container({'type': 'b'})
<BContainer instance>
>>> Container()
<Container instance>
```

There are ways to handle this within a single model as well.

Since `UnionType` doesn't yet support multiple `ModelType` variants, you'll have to do something like this:

```python
class M(Model):
    submodel = PolyModelType([A, B, C], deserialize_from=['a', 'b', 'c'])
```

That's assuming `PolyModelType` is able to identify the correct class from the data alone (the substructure keyed by `'a'` or `'b'` in your example).

If this isn't enough (e.g., you need two-way operation or the data is ambiguous), then it's possible to extend the import machinery and modify the input data before it's operated on (and similarly for outgoing data).

Unfortunately, how exactly to do this is in a bit of a flux right now. I'm working on refactoring the `Model` API as we speak. Something that you can safely do right now is override `Model.__init__` (for incoming data) and `Model.(to_native|to_primitive)` (for outgoing data).

In `__init__`, you could insert a `type` field in the raw data that allows `PolyModelType` to identify the model. Of course, you could also do this in the surrounding code before the source data is even passed to the model constructor.

In the future, you could set something like `app_data={'submodel': B}`, and then use `UnionType` with a custom resolver function like so:

```python
class MultiModelType(UnionType):

    types = (ModelType(A), ModelType(B))

    _type_mapping = {
        A: types[0]
        B: types[1]
    }

    def resolve(self, value, context):
        return self._type_mapping[context.app_data['submodel']]
```

Note that this is just a sketch; I'll have to think on it.

Further testing reveals that this error is only generated on `tox` test runs when modifications have been made to the source file in question. Subsequent `tox` runs on the same source (no modifications) pass without any sign of the error. This indicates that there is some interaction between the way `sqlalchemy` sessions are handled in the test suite and how `tox` caches and installs the repository when changes are made.

Again, this only occurs when Python3.6 is used for testing:

`tox -r -e py36`

For example, testing with Python3.5 after changes have been made generates no error. Immediately testing with Python3.6 after Python3.5 generates the error.

The default (plain) Bibtex defines the abbreviated month as macros. Therefore, it is valid to write `month = jan`.

Even more, the documentation says:

> It’s best to use the three-letter abbreviations for the month, rather than spelling out the month yourself. This lets the bibliography style be consistent. And if you want to include information for the day of the month, the month field is usually the best place. For example
> 
>  `month = jul # "~4,"`
> 
> will probably produce just what you want.

You can check it in the PDF available from the BibTex package http://www.ctan.org/tex-archive/biblio/bibtex/base/

About the formatting, it might worth to make it compliant with what says http://mirrors.ctan.org/biblio/bibtex/base/btxbst.doc (it is a text plain file).

I found a work around. I was getting a cache error when trying to watch  a replay using: python -m pysc2.bin.play --replay <path-to-replay>

To fix it, just load the replay you want to view in the actual SC2 game, and the cache will be built. Then, when you run your command, it will find the cache successfully. It's a pain, but it should be enough to get you going. And there aren't too many maps, so if you can find a replay from each map and load it using the game, you're good to go.

Hi @ashern - thanks for the contribution, sorry we didn't get to it quickly. From a quick glance it looks like a useful thing we'd like to have - as you say, we would be looking for unit tests for any contributed modules.

A reason for the delay on our side is that upstream TF has recently changed how RNNCell works - it now inherits from the base._Layer class which means that any Sonnet RNNCore now effectively implements two RNN APIs which is not a good setup to have. We're looking into how to get around this - most likely changing upstream TF so that dynamic_rnn & the rest don't check for something being an RNNCell, but check for the required functionality (ie a state_size method) being available. This will allow us to make RNNCore not inherit from RNNCell.. At that point, a modified version of your PR would be welcome - I'll let you know when things are stable.

Say you add a BooleanField to a model that controls whether users are allowed to execute a privileged action on a certain object, and default it to False. If you forget to explicitly exclude this field from a form visible to the user, it will now show up as another input ready to be modified and submitted.

If the form data is submitted via AJAX or if the form is rendered field by field, you may not even notice that the new field is now editable.

> Just out of curiosity, is this the expected behavior or will this change in a future version?

I'd think this is expected behavior - with RazorCompileOnBuild or RazorPublishOnBuild enabled, you're effectively getting two binaries per project - one for the app and one for Razor views. They compile different sets of files but we want to share meaningful properties, like versions, between the two assemblies. Our thought was that turning off assembly info for the main one should also disable it for the compiled view, you're more than likely doing something not standard. 

If there's a better approach we could take to addressing this, we'd be happy to consider it. Feel free to file an issue in https://github.com/aspnet/Razor/issues with suggestions.

A popup expects a valid html string. An apostrophe is not valid html, and escaping it in Python is not the same as escaping it in html. Html needs these `'` kind of special character declarations.

* If `my_geojson` features have an attribute `properties.style` expressed in **Leaflet style syntax**, it will be taken into account on the map. It can be used for choropleth, but also for various reasons.
* You can overwrite (`update`) the attributes `properties.style` in providing a styling function : `GeoJson(my_geojson, my_style_function)`.

@altendky It seems that you might have misunderstood what I was suggesting. I was suggesting that a module be added to PySerial that is a higher level API than PySerial that does what you were suggesting. (That is what I meant by on top of PySerial.) This API could end up looking similar in a few places but it could add another layer of things like device capabilities, actual serial protocols like 9 bit A/D, YASP, MODBUS, etc. The issue is that many of these protocols require specific timing and specific control of the serial hardware. This requires actual knowledge of the underlying hardware in some cases to even make it work correctly. PySerial is realistically about the lowest level API that you can go to the serial port and still be pythonic, so it makes sense to set another layer on top of it that gives you more tools and leave the lower level API as stripped down as possible, yet get full control over the hardware. (Again, I am not trying to speak for the PySerial developers, I am just talking from my 35 years of serial protocol experience and a moderate amount of Linux kernel serial driver programming.)

Picking up the thread again, I'm happy to announce Loom 2.0, an almost complete rewrite of loompy. Go to the [release notes](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loompy/releases/tag/v2.0) for the full list of changes. In short, v2.0 implements many of the **features requested** above (such as Unicode support, multidimensional attributes, and multidimensional global attributes). 

It also supports a powerful new concept of **in-memory views** (essentially a slice through the file, including all layers, attributes, and graphs) which works great with the new ``scan()`` method for out-of-memory algorithms.

It is much **more Pythonic**, with a uniform API for reading/writing/deleting attributes, graphs and layers. 

It is **more generous** (allowing almost any kind of list, tuple or array to be assigned to attributes, or any kind of sparse or dense adjacency matrix for graphs). At the same time, it normalizes everything to conform to the file spec.

Finally, I have updated the file format specification to be **more specific** about the allowed datatypes and shapes. 

All this, and it remains **fully backwards compatible with your old loom files**. I have used 2.0 for a couple of months already and it's a significant step forward. Your code will be simpler, more expressive and more capable. 

Note: your current code should mostly work without problems. You'll get log messages for deprecated methods and attributes, but they can be safely ignored. There are **two breaking changes**, both mentioned in the release notes. After fixing instances of these two issues, our current analysis pipeline runs without error and uses nearly every feature of loompy 2.0, so I would consider this a reasonably stable release. Out-of-memory manifold learning and clustering of a 500k cells by 27k genes dataset with all the bells and whistles runs just fine on my MacBook with 16 GB RAM (admittedly, it takes a few hours)!

I've **updated the docs**, which are now (for technical reasons) hosted at http://linnarssonlab.org/loompy.

Thanks everyone for your thoughts on this issue.

I'm now seeing almost unanimous support for independent ``PVSystem`` and ``Location`` classes, so let's go in that direction first. I updated #93 based on this conclusion. I think that the ``Location`` class in #93 is ok (though not yet complete). The implementation details of the ``PVSystem`` class in #93 need more thought. I'll welcome pull requests to [my branch](https://github.com/wholmgren/pvlib-python/tree/pvsystem) or competing pull requests to pvlib/master.

@jforbess: my understanding is that you would have favored a single class that combines it all. It lieu of that we can also experiment with a ``LocalizedPVSystem`` that inherits from both. I *think* that it would be fairly easy to implement, but I don't have much experience with multiple inheritance. The internet is full of "never use multiple inheritance" advice, but I think that it could work out ok in this case. The following code seems to prove that it's possible to have reuse the same base ``PVSystem`` and ``Location`` classes in a composite ``LocalizedPVSystem`` class:

```python
class PVSystem(object):
    def __init__(self, module, inverter, **kwargs):
        print('initializing a PVSystem')
        self.module = module
        self.inverter = inverter 
        super(PVSystem, self).__init__(**kwargs)

class Location(object):
    def __init__(self, latitude, longitude, **kwargs):
        print('initializing a Location')
        self.latitude = latitude 
        self.longitude = longitude 
        super(Location, self).__init__(**kwargs)

# inheritance order should not matter if defined as above
class LocalizedPVSystem(PVSystem, Location):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        print('initializing a LocalizedPVSystem')
        super(LocalizedPVSystem, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        print('LocalizedPVSystem initialized')

In [17]: localized_pv_system = LocalizedPVSystem(latitude=30, longitude=-110, module='blah', inverter='blarg')
initializing a LocalizedPVSystem
initializing a PVSystem
initializing a Location
LocalizedPVSystem initialized

In [18]: localized_pv_system.latitude
Out[18]: 30

In [19]: localized_pv_system.module
Out[19]: 'blah'

In [21]: pv_system = PVSystem(module='blah', inverter='blarg')
initializing a PVSystem

In [22]: pv_system.module
Out[22]: 'blah'

In [23]: location = Location(latitude=30, longitude=-110)
initializing a Location

In [24]: location.latitude
Out[24]: 30
```

I do think that some people would find this to be a more useful paradigm than a combination of ``Location``, ``PVSystem``, and ``ModelChain``. So, I am in favor of supporting both approaches provided that it's not too much extra work.

For your Problem No.1: 

- If you are switching to a simple camera plus openCV solution, my answer would be to do it the dumb way: First you may define some pixel position, say [320, 240] if your camera is 640×480, in your field of view as the current position of your drone. If the line is detected on your right, change you roll value (with caution) like the way you control the drone with your RC transmitter, the vice versa. Remember to set a zone of tolerance, which means if the line is not too right or too left, tell the drone to "consider it done" and you may change the pitch value to tell it go forward or backward during this period.
- If you are using a camera, a optical flow sensor (possibly the same camera ? ), and a lidar, you can still do it the dumb way (which can already be a huge success after some debugging). But how about study your lidar or optical flow sensor more closely, understand how they are working with your flight control, what value are they sending to your flight control board ? If you are capable of editing and compiling your DIY version of your firmware, how about enabling it to handle the information you retrieved with your camera ? If not, how about send the information in the same way your optical flow sensor or Gps module send them ? So your flight control board can do it the smarter way rather than simply following the orders of their master?

Hello @ljc19800331,
There seem to be several questions entangled:
>question 1: Is the color frame from the stereo camera different from the middle camera (The camera that takes the standard image)

SR300 device has two sensors: Depth/IR and RGB. The third "eye" in the front panel is the structured light pattern  projector. So the meaning of `...color frame from the stereo` and `...middle camera` is not clear. Please elaborate

>question 2: Is there a good way to choose a random region in the image and return the corresponding 3D point sets in python?

In case you refer to choosing a random region in RGB image, then using the `align` object will allow to obtain the corresponding depth region in a straight-forwarded manner. Note that generating pointcloud from "depth aligned to color" will produce slightly different results when compared with pointcloud from original depth frame. 
This is due to the fact that "aligned depth" pixels must reside on the grid of the reference (color) frame.
And rounding-up/down depth coordinates during texture mapping will manifest in certain misalignment when converting "Aligned depth" 2D image to 3D pointcloud.

I believe SR300 reports temperatures using same API as F200. Check GetIRTemp = 0x52 and GetMEMSTemp = 0x0A USB commands and the related read_ir_temp and read_mems_temp functions. 
There are couple of changes that might be relevant: First, when camera is not streaming depth, F200 would successfully return temperature 0, while SR300 will fail with 'Invalid HW State" code. 
Another difference is that the temperature control loop in F200 is done in software, while SR300 is handling that automatically in hardware.

In my opinion, we should keep VM-management logic out of Pulp Smash.

Right now, Pulp Smash has a pretty well defined interface. Users can tell Pulp Smash what system(s) are available via (by default) a `~/.config/pulp_smash/settings.json` file, and Pulp Smash can choose which tests to run based on what's available. Easy enough. This well defined interface has at least the following benefits:

* Pulp Smash doesn't care about the nature of target systems: they can be VMs backed by libvirt, virtualbox, vmware, xen, etc, or docker containers, or bare-metal systems, or the local system, or even a mixture of these. This imparts a great deal of flexibility. Have you managed to get Pulp running on your gasoline-powered cheese grater? Great! Pulp Smash can test that system.
* Pulp Smash doesn't care where it's running from. Can you get the dependencies installed and provide password-less shell access to the target systems? Great! Pulp Smash can run on the current system.
* This well defined interface prevents scope creep. Pulp Smash is already able to talk to multiple systems via their API and CLI, and it does things like stop and start system services, drop databases, delete files from their filesystems, and so on. This is a reasonable level of complexity. Including VM management logic would necessarily add more complexity to Pulp Smash.

Now, I totally agree that it'd be nice to have multi-machine testing, where the environment is programmatically managed. So - how can we do that and still integrate nicely with Pulp Smash?

Well - use the `settings.json` interface! It's plain old JSON, and any programming language that speaks JSON can munge that file. Even better, if the tool that's managing VMs is Python (or has Python bindings?) (or can run `python -c '...'` commands?), have that tool use `pulp_smash.config.ServerConfig` to manage the `settings.json` file.

I agree with @patrick91. I believe the error fields on the SerializerMutation are quite intentionally separate from GraphQL errors. These are generally field-level validation errors and will not only have a different structure from normal errors, but are handled differently by the client.

For example, getting a GraphQL query syntax error is not your end-user's fault, and should be handled by a developer before you get into production. However, the "errors" on the SerializerMutation will happen all the time in production and should be passed on to your end user so that they can fix the data in their form and try again.

> Do we still want to throw SendErrors in other cases? Seems like all of these are undesirable from a customer perspective.

I think we do not want to throw SendErrors in those cases either, and I [recently changed libhoney-rb](https://github.com/honeycombio/libhoney-rb/pull/25) to fall back to a no-op in those cases. However, the user story there is "remembered to initialize libhoney but didn't fill in some of its config, then tried to send events", which is slightly less clear-cut than "forgot to initialize libhoney at all, then tried to send events". So I suggest leaving that change out of the scope of this PR.

There's a broader conversation to be had about the SDK, how we handle misconfiguration, and how we support certain scenarios where you omit the write key and/or dataset from the `.Init` call (or `Client` instantiation) and specify them at the builder level instead. My libhoney-rb change linked above jumped the gun and presupposed a conclusion to that conversation, but [Ben pointed out](https://houndsh.slack.com/archives/C4M02EG83/p1528221051000272) that makes it inconsistent with the other SDKs and with our [SDK spec](https://docs.honeycomb.io/reference/sdk-spec/). I have a todo item to start an email thread to discuss this.

@jwhui  - i have a slightly different proposal that I think is more portable.

I also believe this is a less invasive way to solve the problem

**PART 1**  In the file:  "openthread-core-<CHIPNAMEHERE>-config.h"  define a pair of macros

About the MACRO name:

I don't think the macro name should be "CLOCK HIGH" or "CLOCK LOW" , or "COMMISSION START"  and "COMMISSION STOP" - there could very well be other reasons that a clock might need to change speeds.  What about the time when the device is waiting for the next packet to come over the air? 

I think think something like:  OPENTHREAD_POWER_BEGIN(REASON) and OPENTHREAD_END(REASON)  I'm sure there will be *other* power issues that come up later - hence the idea of a REASON code.

For now we three reasons,   IDLE, NORMAL_WORK, CRYPTO_DTLS

**PART 2**  In the file:  "openthread-core-default-config.h" - which provides the defaults...

Provide a default macros for OPENTHREAD_POWER_BEGIN()  and OPENTHREAD_POWER_END() that does nothing.  Thus platforms that do not need, or do not want this can reasonably ignore these.

**PART 3** - bracket the calls to the MBED DTLS crypto library - with a BEGIN/END macro.
I think this would be entirely in the file:  src/thread/meshcop/dtls.cpp

I would be very judicious about where I turned this on, and turned it off.  For example you don't want it on anytime a DTLS session is active, you only want it on during the time computations are occuring.

**PART 4** Another approach is to look at *selective* optimization of code in the MBED DTLS files.

Generally, OpenThread is compiled with "-Os" - meaning to hell with speed, keep space at a premium, and however the mbed: "bignum.c" file is the beast.   Selecting *different* optimization in a ./configure based makefile is a PITA.  And gets complicated.

An idea, (i've been experimenting with, and still working on, so don't count on it any time soon) is to use a GCC pragma like below, inserted via a  "PATCH" for the "bignum.c" file, something like this at the top of the file: "bignum.c".   The difference is:  "-Os" (space) = bignum is about 7K bytes of code, "-Ofast" it ballons to about 15K bytes of code.

```
#pragma GCC  optimize("-Ofast")
```

fastText do use newlines to separate examples (for both supervised and unsupervised modes). Thus, when learning word vectors using `skipgram` or `cbow`, the words from the previous and next lines do not influence the learning of the current line. See [line 347](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/src/dictionary.cc#L347) of dictionary.cc for the corresponding code (newlines are replaced by `EOS` in the readWord method).

There are plenty of stuff that won't work after a `optimize()`. We had this very same issue in [CSIP](https://github.com/leethargo/CSIP) and we decided to call `freeTransform()` right after `optimize`:
```C
    SCIP_in_CSIP(SCIPsolve(model->scip));
    model->status = getStatus(model);
    SCIP_in_CSIP(SCIPfreeTransform(model->scip));
```
Now, storing *only* the status is far from correct and I guess we don't really want to the same here.

As far as I see, there are two solution:
1 We call `freeTransform` at the beginning of any function that will not work after a `SCIPsolve`
2 We have a method, say something like, `reset` which calls `freeTransform` and then allows the user to modify the problem and solve again.

Thoughts?

> which means for me as a user that I sleep a few seconds, and then try the 2nd call again.

That is what I mean it moves the timeout to the second API call, waiting a few seconds before the next request is just a different form of a timeout. With this approach one needs to choose how often to retry (with backing-off or not) and how many retries prior to considering a failure.

Ideally we should let the user know the software didn't hang, but we cant do that with the HTTP transport. For the moment the goal is to make these APIs blocking and idempotent, allowing the user to choose its timeout and to safely retry.

@rakanalh hahaha nice find. So basically the function **should** have been doing what the comment says it does. But as you noticed, due to using `views.get_channelstate_for_receiving` it is doing the exact opposite. That is definitely a bug.

But let's take a step back and explain the reasoning here. In the past we could not recover from an irregular shutdown. Now with the WAL we can and we do so.

Instead what we guaranteed was that you safely use Raiden and shut it down regularly. Shutting down regurarly would cause you to close all channels and only after closing and settling would the shutdown be succesfull. To make that faster while keeping the safety guarantee we added this "only_receiving" flag to do what the comment describes. But it should have been `only_not_received` instead.

Now that we are done with the history lesson ... I could go as far as saying we don't really need that argument anymore. So I would remove that logic completely, especially considering it's not exposed from the web UI.

Actually, @CyrusNajmabadi, on second thought, I think it would be better to have a small number of lines (perhaps even just one line for the current operation) displaying what is currently happening, as the resource is being initialized.

A good example of where this is useful is when Pulumi is polling repeatedly because it doesn't have access to some container registry; this could change, but it probably won't, and displaying this to the user is useful, because it gives them the information they need to decide to cancel the rollout and fix the issue themselves.

And on a _muuuuuuch_ less scientific note, which I present less as a strong suggestion but more because I happen to like the look of a (slightly?) busier output screen, and I'm going to shamelessly cherry-pick things to support my hypothesis: there's this trick travel websites do to increase engagement. They find that putting a loading screen before travel results actually causes people to value the results more. This is why even new sites like Hipmunk have a loading bar, even though it takes a very small amount of time to execute the search.

As I said, not a suggestion I expect you to consider extremely carefully, but perhaps worth some brief consideration. :)

I'm having trouble understanding the use case... you have a trio program, that does a blocking/synchronous call to `IPython.embed`, and then inside that blocking call you want to do another call to `trio.run`, so the user can use direct `await` calls inside the embedded interpreter *but* these `await` calls are running on an entirely different loop than the main program, so if you they try to actually `await` any of the stuff in the program you're embedded in then things will explode dramatically... this seems like it will confuse people a lot, and not be very useful?

I can understand why you'd want to do a regular synchronous `IPython.embed` to freeze an async program and poke around in it, without async support inside the embedded interpreter. And I can see why you'd want to support some kind of `await IPython.async_embed()` that re-uses the ambient loop, so you can interactively explore the async program and do stuff like call `await that_queue.put(...)` to poke at the live code while it runs. But I don't get why nesting a second `run` would be what you want (either for trio or for other loops).

Am I missing something?

> How can I return a Scala equivalent of this data frame

The only way in the current version of Jep would be to make a Scala/Java object, import it and instantiate it in Python, and then set it up by invoking Scala/Java methods on it.  When getting a value out of Python, Jep will turn it into a String of the object if all its built-in conversion methods fail.  That is issue #43.

> Is there a way to return a reference only (to the data frame) that Python would recognize if I sent it back from Scala in a subsequent call?

In the current version of Jep, no.

> Is there a way to define custom converters?

At present only if you do as I state above and have a Scala/Java object and write your own conversion code in Python.

Ideally we would return a pointer to a Python object that provides some features and could be passed back into Python.  I have an experimental branch on my fork that supports that.  https://github.com/ndjensen/jep/tree/dev_4.0/src/main/java/jep/python  You might play with that and provide feedback if you think it should make it into Jep.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import tensorflow as tf
import tensorlayer as tl

tf.logging.set_verbosity(tf.logging.DEBUG)
tl.logging.set_verbosity(tl.logging.DEBUG)

# 1) simple dynamic rnn
input_seqs = tf.placeholder(dtype=tf.int64, shape=[None, None], name="input")
is_train = True
ne = tl.layers.EmbeddingInputlayer(inputs=input_seqs, vocabulary_size=100, embedding_size=100, name='embedding')
# inside DynamicRNNLayer, batch_size need to be computed automatically from previous outputs.
# there are also many cases that require tensor from previous layers as input of next layer
fixed_batch_size = ne.outputs.get_shape().with_rank_at_least(1)[0]
if fixed_batch_size.value:
    batch_size = fixed_batch_size.value
else:
    from tensorflow.python.ops import array_ops
    batch_size = array_ops.shape(ne.outputs)[0]
print(batch_size)
n = tl.layers.DynamicRNNLayer(
    ne,
    cell_fn=tf.contrib.rnn.BasicLSTMCell,
    n_hidden=100,
    dropout=(0.7 if is_train else None),
    sequence_length=tl.layers.retrieve_seq_length_op2(input_seqs),  # previous tensor is required
    return_last=False,
    return_seq_2d=True,
    name='dynamicrnn'
)
n = tl.layers.DenseLayer(n, n_units=100, name="output")
print(input_seqs, ne.outputs)

# 2) intermedia tensor
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 100, 100, 3], name='x')

nin = tl.layers.InputLayer(x, name='in')
n1 = tl.layers.Conv2d(nin, 32, (3, 3), (1, 1), act=tf.nn.relu, name='c1')
n1 = tl.layers.MaxPool2d(n1, (3, 3), (2, 2), 'SAME', name='pad1')
n1 = tl.layers.Conv2d(n1, 32, (3, 3), (1, 1), act=tf.nn.relu, name='c2')
print(n1.all_layers)  # intermedia layers should have this before compiling
print(n1.all_params)  # not only for debugging, but for building complex network
print(n1.outputs)     # I can give more examples later

n2 = tl.layers.Conv2d(nin, 32, (3, 3), (1, 1), act=tf.nn.relu, name='c1')
n2 = tl.layers.MaxPool2d(n2, (3, 3), (2, 2), 'SAME', name='pad1')
n2 = tl.layers.Conv2d(n2, 32, (3, 3), (1, 1), act=tf.nn.relu, name='c2')
print(n1.all_layers)
print(n1.all_params)
print(n1.outputs)

n = tl.layers.ElementwiseLayer([n1, n2], tf.add, name='add')

# 3) tl.models
x2 = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 224, 224, 3])
# get VGG without the last layer
vgg = tl.models.VGG16(x2, end_with='fc2_relu')
# add one more layer
n3 = tl.layers.DenseLayer(vgg, 100, name='out')

# initialize all parameters
sess = tf.Session()
sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())

# download and restore vgg paramters online
vgg.restore_params(sess)    # vgg need all_params to restore params
train_params = tl.layers.get_variables_with_name('out')
print(train_params)

```
